class_schedule.topic: What Drives Innovation? Frameworks for Understanding Technological Change
learning_objectives: Define innovation and distinguish it from invention
Apply the S-curve model of technological adoption
Identify the social, economic, and political factors that enable or hinder innovation
readings: The Innovators Ch. 1 by Walter Isaacson
The Structure of Scientific Revolutions Ch. 1-3 by Thomas Kuhn (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Innovation is not just about inventing something new. It is about translating an invention into widespread use that changes how people live. Understanding the patterns of innovation helps us anticipate what comes next and why.
key_concepts: Invention (creating something new) vs. innovation (making it useful and widespread)
The S-curve: slow start, rapid growth, maturity, decline
Disruptive vs. sustaining innovation (Christensen)
Enabling conditions: capital, education, freedom, demand, and infrastructure
Path dependence: how early choices constrain future options (QWERTY)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping the S-curve of smartphone adoption using real market data from 2007-2023
setup_requirements: Access to Statista or Our World in Data for adoption curves

review.value: New technologies follow a predictable pattern: slow adoption by innovators, rapid growth as the mainstream adopts, then saturation. Understanding where a technology sits on the curve informs investment and strategy.

Early design choices become locked in as ecosystems build around them. The QWERTY keyboard, VHS over Betamax, and gasoline engines over electric in the early 1900s are all examples of path dependence.

learning_objectives: Explain how the Agricultural Revolution transformed human society
Analyze the causes and consequences of the Industrial Revolution
Evaluate the human costs and benefits of industrialization
readings: Sapiens Ch. 5-6 by Yuval Noah Harari
The Industrial Revolution: A Very Short Introduction Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Two revolutions fundamentally reshaped human civilization. The Agricultural Revolution (circa 10,000 BCE) made cities possible. The Industrial Revolution (circa 1760-1840) made mass production possible. Both came with enormous benefits and enormous costs.
key_concepts: Agricultural Revolution: domestication, surplus, specialization, cities
Industrial Revolution: steam power, mechanization, factory system
Enabling factors: coal, capital, colonies, canals, and patents
Social consequences: urbanization, child labor, pollution, wealth inequality
The Luddites: technology resistance as rational response to displacement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing productivity, working conditions, and living standards before and after industrialization using primary source documents
setup_requirements: Primary source excerpts: factory inspectors' reports, worker testimonies (provided)

review.value: James Watt's improved steam engine (1769) converted coal into mechanical energy, breaking the dependence on human, animal, wind, and water power. It enabled factories, railways, and steamships.

Often mischaracterized as anti-technology, Luddites were skilled workers protesting the destruction of their livelihoods by machines. Their concerns about automation displacing workers remain relevant today.

class_schedule.topic: Electricity, Communication, and the Second Industrial Revolution
learning_objectives: Trace the development of electricity from laboratory curiosity to universal utility
Explain how the telegraph and telephone transformed communication and business
Analyze Edison's innovation model and the rise of industrial research laboratories
readings: The Innovators Ch. 2 by Walter Isaacson
Empires of Light Ch. 1-4 by Jill Jonnes (selected)

lecture_notes.introduction: The Second Industrial Revolution (1870-1914) was powered by electricity and driven by systematic innovation. Edison did not just invent the light bulb; he invented the entire system of electrical generation and distribution that made it useful.
key_concepts: From Faraday's experiments to Edison's power grid: science to system
Edison vs. Tesla: the War of Currents (DC vs. AC)
The telegraph: the Victorian internet that shrank the world
Bell and the telephone: from novelty to necessity
The industrial research laboratory: systematizing innovation (Menlo Park, Bell Labs)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Timeline construction: mapping the cascade of innovations that electricity enabled (light, motors, refrigeration, radio)
setup_requirements: Primary source: Edison's patent for the phonograph

review.value: Edison's genius was not the light bulb alone but the entire system: generators, wiring, meters, switches, and power stations. Innovation at the system level is more impactful than any single device.

Before the telegraph (1844), information traveled at the speed of a horse. After, it traveled at the speed of electricity. This transformed financial markets, journalism, warfare, and railroad operations overnight.

class_schedule.topic: The Computer Revolution: From Babbage to the Personal Computer
learning_objectives: Trace the lineage of computing from mechanical to electronic to personal
Identify the key figures and breakthroughs in computer development
Analyze why certain innovations succeeded while others were forgotten
readings: The Innovators Ch. 3-7 by Walter Isaacson
The Mother of All Demos (Douglas Engelbart, 1968, summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: The computer is arguably the most transformative technology in human history. But it was not invented by one person in one moment. It emerged through decades of contributions from mathematicians, engineers, and visionaries, many of whom were forgotten by history.
key_concepts: Ada Lovelace and Charles Babbage: the first concept of a programmable machine
Alan Turing: the theoretical foundation of computation
ENIAC and the first electronic computers: World War II as innovation catalyst
The transistor (1947) and integrated circuit (1958): miniaturization begins
The personal computer revolution: Xerox PARC, Apple, and Microsoft
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing the computational power of ENIAC (1945) to a modern smartphone and discussing what enabled the transformation
setup_requirements: Video clips of Engelbart's 1968 demo (links provided)

review.value: Turing's 1936 paper proved that a single universal machine could compute anything computable by following instructions on a tape. This theoretical foundation underlies every computer ever built.

Invented the graphical user interface, the mouse, Ethernet, and laser printing in the 1970s. Xerox failed to commercialize them; Apple and Microsoft did. Innovation requires not just invention but also commercialization and timing.

class_schedule.topic: The Internet, the Web, and the Platform Economy
learning_objectives: Distinguish between the internet (infrastructure) and the web (application)
Trace the evolution from ARPANET to the modern platform economy
Analyze how network effects create winner-take-all markets
readings: The Innovators Ch. 8-11 by Walter Isaacson
Platform Revolution Ch. 1-2 by Parker, Van Alstyne & Choudary

lecture_notes.introduction: The internet began as a military research project and became the infrastructure for the global economy. The web, built on top of the internet, democratized publishing. Platforms, built on top of the web, created entirely new kinds of businesses and social structures.
key_concepts: ARPANET (1969): packet switching and decentralized network design
Tim Berners-Lee and the World Wide Web (1991): HTML, URLs, HTTP
The dot-com boom and bust: irrational exuberance and the survivors
Web 2.0 and user-generated content: Wikipedia, YouTube, social media
Network effects: why platforms become natural monopolies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping the evolution from ARPANET's four nodes to the modern internet using interactive timeline tools

review.value: Instead of a dedicated circuit for each communication (like a phone call), data is broken into packets routed independently across the network and reassembled at the destination. This makes the network resilient and efficient.

A product becomes more valuable as more people use it. Facebook is useful because your friends are there; they are there because it is useful. This creates a flywheel that leads to market dominance and high barriers to entry.

class_schedule.topic: The Future of Innovation: AI, Biotech, and What Comes Next
learning_objectives: Identify the most significant emerging technologies and their potential impacts
Apply historical patterns of innovation to predict adoption trajectories
Evaluate the ethical and social questions that new technologies raise
readings: The Innovators Epilogue by Walter Isaacson
The Coming Wave Ch. 1-3 by Mustafa Suleyman

lecture_notes.introduction: History does not predict the future, but it reveals patterns. Every major technology has followed similar trajectories of hype, disillusionment, and eventual transformation. Understanding these patterns helps us think clearly about what comes next.
key_concepts: Artificial intelligence: from expert systems to large language models
Biotechnology: CRISPR gene editing and synthetic biology
Quantum computing: potential and current limitations
The Gartner Hype Cycle: peak of inflated expectations to plateau of productivity
The innovation dilemma: powerful technologies require powerful governance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students place current technologies on the Gartner Hype Cycle and debate their positions

review.value: Technology trigger > Peak of inflated expectations > Trough of disillusionment > Slope of enlightenment > Plateau of productivity. Most technologies follow this pattern; understanding it prevents both hype and premature dismissal.

Every transformative technology (printing press, steam, electricity, internet, AI) has followed a similar arc: initial hype, social disruption, institutional adaptation, and eventually profound normalization. The transition period is always chaotic.

questions.question: Explain the difference between invention and innovation using a specific historical example. Why did the innovation matter more than the invention?
questions.answer: Invention is creating something new; innovation is translating that creation into widespread, practical use. Example: The electric light bulb was invented by several people before Edison, but Edison innovated by creating the entire electrical system needed to make it useful: reliable generators, safe wiring, metering, and power distribution infrastructure. The bulb alone was a curiosity; the system changed civilization. The innovation mattered more because without the supporting infrastructure, the invention could not reach or benefit anyone. Innovation bridges the gap between a working prototype and societal transformation.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of invention and innovation

questions.question: Describe path dependence and explain how it applies to a technology we still use today despite better alternatives being available.
questions.answer: Path dependence occurs when early choices become locked in because ecosystems, habits, infrastructure, and standards build around them, making switching to alternatives prohibitively costly even when better options exist. Example: The QWERTY keyboard layout was designed in the 1870s to prevent mechanical typewriter key jams by separating frequently used letter pairs. This constraint is irrelevant for electronic keyboards, and alternative layouts like Dvorak have been shown to be faster. But QWERTY persists because billions of people have learned it, every keyboard is manufactured with it, every typing course teaches it, and the switching cost for society would be enormous for a modest speed improvement.

rubric.key: Valid modern example with historical origin
Explains why switching is difficult despite better alternatives

questions.question: Explain why Xerox PARC is considered one of the greatest examples of invention without innovation. What did they invent, and why did they fail to capitalize on it?
questions.answer: Xerox PARC (Palo Alto Research Center) in the 1970s invented the graphical user interface with windows and icons, the computer mouse, Ethernet networking, laser printing, and object-oriented programming. These inventions became the foundation of the modern computing industry. Xerox failed to capitalize because: (1) Corporate leadership in New York did not understand the research and saw Xerox as a copier company, not a computer company. (2) There was no organizational pathway to bring research to market. (3) Management failed to recognize the commercial potential of what their researchers had built. Apple's Steve Jobs visited PARC in 1979, recognized the potential immediately, and built the Macintosh around these ideas. This case illustrates that innovation requires not just invention but organizational alignment, market vision, and the will to commercialize.

rubric.key: Lists at least three PARC inventions
Explains why Xerox failed to commercialize
Draws lesson about innovation vs. invention

questions.question: What are network effects, and how do they explain why a few technology platforms (Google, Amazon, Facebook) dominate their markets?
questions.answer: Network effects occur when a product or service becomes more valuable as more people use it. Direct network effects: a social network is more useful when your friends are on it. Indirect network effects: more iPhone users attract more app developers, which makes iPhones more useful, which attracts more users. These effects explain platform dominance because once a platform reaches critical mass, the value gap between it and competitors widens with every new user. New entrants face a chicken-and-egg problem: they cannot attract users without content/network, and cannot build content/network without users. This creates winner-take-all dynamics where the first platform to achieve scale in a category tends to maintain dominance, making markets naturally monopolistic.

rubric.key: Correct definition of network effects with examples
Explains how network effects create market dominance
Discusses barriers to entry for competitors

major_assignments.title: Innovation Case Study: From Invention to Impact
major_assignments.description: Select a significant technological innovation (not covered in class lectures) and write a comprehensive case study tracing its journey from initial invention through widespread adoption. Apply the innovation frameworks from the course to analyze what enabled its success and what obstacles it overcame.
objectives: Research the full history of a technology from concept to mainstream adoption
Apply course frameworks: S-curve, disruptive vs. sustaining innovation, path dependence, network effects
Identify the social, economic, and political factors that shaped the technology's trajectory
Draw lessons from history that apply to current and emerging technologies
deliverables: Innovation timeline visual showing key milestones
S-curve analysis with adoption data if available
Framework application section using at least three course concepts
Lessons for today: what does this case teach us about current innovations?
Bibliography with at least 6 sources including primary and secondary

criteria: Thorough, accurate history from invention to adoption
Key figures and their contributions identified
Context provided: what was happening socially, economically, politically
Primary and secondary sources used
At least three course frameworks applied correctly
Frameworks illuminate the case rather than being applied superficially
Analysis explains why the technology succeeded or evolved as it did
Counterexamples or limitations of frameworks acknowledged
Clear connections drawn to current technologies
Lessons are specific and actionable, not vague platitudes
Historical parallels identified thoughtfully
Nuance and humility about prediction acknowledged
Clear, engaging writing
Timeline visual is informative and well-designed
Proper citation format throughout
Error-free and professionally formatted
Comprehensive, well-researched case study of a significant innovation
Course frameworks applied with genuine analytical depth
Meaningful lessons drawn for contemporary technology landscape
Professional writing and presentation throughout

participation_and_discussion.description: Active engagement in class discussions and group activities

quizzes.description: Regular assessment of historical knowledge and analytical frameworks

weekly_innovation_journal.description: Weekly journal entries applying historical frameworks to current technology news

case_study.description: In-depth innovation case study with framework analysis and contemporary lessons

final_exam.description: Comprehensive exam covering major innovations, frameworks, and analytical skills

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected for discussion participation. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce the participation grade by 5% per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Journal entries are due weekly and cannot be submitted late. The case study has staged deadlines with 10% per day late penalty on the final submission.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussions are collaborative. Written work must be individual. You may discuss ideas with classmates but must write your analysis independently.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citation. Fabricating historical facts or sources will result in a zero and academic integrity referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Internet access for research. No specialized software required. Access to JSTOR and other academic databases through the university library.
